results.coaching_summary: Strong, clean handling of a Tier-1 case. AE/PC Detection: the trainee correctly caught the volunteered cue (sinus infection/antibiotic while on Cardizan) and appropriately captured/routed it as an intercurrent AE to PV without fabricating or over-probing for additional symptoms — exactly the listen-and-clarify standard required. Questioning Technique: good use of an open clarifying question to establish requester type, and correct restraint in not fishing for symptoms beyond what was volunteered. Compliance: response stayed strictly within the approved SRL content (drug-interaction classes affecting INR), no medical advice or off-label content, no promotional language. Documentation: all required fields (contact, product, inquiry summary, AE description, SRL citation, response route, routing) were completed accurately and consistently with the transcript; received date and category correctly logged. Empathy: professional, warm, and respectful tone throughout with no condescension.

results.coaching_summary: Overall a well-handled interaction with strong service fundamentals, correct product/SRL selection, and appropriate routing of the true adverse event (sinus infection during Cardizan therapy) — including obtaining HCP consent to be listed as reporter. However, a critical Regulatory risk / AE Detection issue occurred: after the caller had already said goodbye, the trainee re-opened the call to fish for unraised symptoms ('have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?'), eliciting a vague, unconfirmed 'maybe a mild headache' that the caller explicitly said she wasn't calling about. This is solicitation, not listen-and-clarify, and it led to documenting a fabricated/unsupported AE in place of the actual ground-truth AE (the sinus infection), which was never entered in the ae_description field at all. This is a significant documentation and detection accuracy failure.

results.coaching_summary: Strong overall handling of an ambiguous-requester efficacy call that concealed a lack-of-effect signal. AE/PC Detection: the trainee correctly caught the volunteered "not much relief yet" cue, flagged it as a potential lack-of-effect AE and quality complaint, and routed dual to PV and Quality as required — matching the ground truth exactly. Questioning Technique: the trainee used a clean, non-solicitous clarifying question to establish requester type (patient vs. HCP) rather than probing for symptoms, which is exactly the "listen-and-clarify" skill being trained, and did not fabricate any additional AE beyond what was raised. Compliance: no off-label question arose so S5.1 is n/a; the trainee proactively and appropriately redirected the caller to their pharmacist rather than advising a dose change themselves, which protects against medical-advice risk. Documentation: nearly all required fields were completed correctly (lot, expiration, NDC, sample availability, AE/PC descriptions, dual routing flags, SRL citation, HCP consent) and the resolution/closure checklist accurately reflects the call outcome.

results.coaching_summary: Overall a strong, compliant handling of an ambiguous-requester efficacy call with an embedded lack-of-effect AE/PC signal. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S3.1/S5.2): the trainee correctly caught the volunteered lack-of-effect cue without fishing for additional symptoms, flagged it appropriately for PV, and correctly identified the parallel product-quality angle, routing to both PV and Quality as required. Questioning Technique (S1.3): the trainee properly clarified requester type and clarified the ambiguous cue the caller raised, rather than probing for unraised AEs — exactly the listen-and-clarify approach expected. Compliance (S5.1/S5.3): no off-label material arose, and the response stayed balanced and non-promotional, correctly deferring dosing questions to the caller's pharmacist. Documentation (S2.4-S2.10, S3.4-S3.12, S4): most required fields (lot, expiration, NDC, availability, dual routing flags, SRL, contact info) are complete and accurate; however, the PC description was not captured as its own distinct entry — the safety/PC narrative only exists in the AE field, so the documentation does not clearly delineate the product-quality complaint description separately as required. In a live QA environment this could cause the Quality team to receive an incomplete or ambiguous PC narrative since it's commingled with the AE text. Empathy (S1.2): tone was professional and warm throughout. Regulatory risk (S5 criticals): dual routing was correctly executed and documented, keeping regulatory exposure low.

results.coaching_summary: This case was handled well on requester clarification, AE/PC identification (S2.1/S3.1), and dual routing to PV and Quality (S2.3/S3.3/S5.4) — all correctly documented per the answer key. However, a critical Regulatory Risk failure occurred at line [13]: after correctly delivering the approved SRL response and appropriately directing the caller to their pharmacist before changing dose, the trainee then directly contradicted this guidance by telling the caller to 'just double the dose to get faster relief.' This is unsolicited, non-compliant medical/dosing advice that: (1) violates S4.2's no-medical-advice standard, (2) breaches on-inquiry scope/conciseness under S4.5 by volunteering unsafe guidance beyond the approved response, (3) reads as promotional/unbalanced under S5.3, and (4) undermines the HCP-referral safety net required under S5.5. This is a serious, reportable compliance breach in a live QA environment and would likely trigger escalation/retraining regardless of the otherwise strong documentation quality.

results.coaching_summary: This call was handled well on the customer-service side (S1) — professional tone, correct requester-type clarification, and a compliant response that avoided medical advice and redirected the caller to their pharmacist for dosing. However, the case reveals a critical AE/PC Detection gap: the caller explicitly volunteered a 4-day history of no symptom relief, which per the ground truth constitutes both a reportable 'lack of effect' AE and a related potential product-quality complaint requiring dual routing to PV and Quality. The trainee's verbal response acknowledged the onset-timing issue appropriately, but this was never translated into the required safety documentation — ae_present, pc_present, and special_situations were all left as 'no'/empty, and PV/Quality routing flags were both false. This is a classic 'said the right thing, didn't document it' failure: the conversation-level handling doesn't compensate for the missing case record, and in a live QA environment this would be scored as a critical miss with real regulatory/pharmacovigilance risk exposure (an actual lack-of-effect signal would go unreported to PV and unrouted to Quality for lot investigation).

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance overall. AE/PC Detection: the trainee excelled at listen-and-clarify — catching the caller's offhand, half-finished 'shaky' remark mid-sentence and asking for specifics (onset, whether ongoing) without turning it into a probing interrogation. This is exactly the target behavior and surfaced a real AE (tremor/palpitations) that would otherwise have been missed. Compliance: consent to contact the HCP was properly obtained and documented, no off-label content arose, and the response stayed strictly on the administration question with no promotional or advisory language on symptom management — the caller was correctly referred to the prescriber. Documentation: nearly all required fields were completed correctly (contact info, AE description, SRL citation, routing to PV), matching the transcript closely. The one gap is in the resolution/completion notes for the AE — the case lacks a clear closing note documenting that the AE was captured, routed to PV, and next steps, which should be added to close the loop for audit purposes.

inquiry.summary:
A caregiver calls with what sounds like a routine question about their spouse's inhaler technique.
The caller **volunteers** an offhand mention of a symptom (the patient has been "a bit shaky") that they
do **not** frame as a problem. The core MI skill is **listening and clarifying, not probing**: the
specialist must catch the volunteered cue and clarify its specifics — never interrogate for symptoms the
caller never raised. If the cue is let pass, the AE is missed, exactly like a real call.

results.coaching_summary: This case centered on a classic 'listen-and-clarify, not probe' skill test, and the trainee missed the critical cue entirely. The caller volunteered an offhand remark about the patient being 'shaky' (per the reveal rule), which was meant to surface a genuine adverse event (tremor + palpitations, onset ~1 week post-initiation). The trainee answered only the surface-level administration question (mouth rinsing) and closed the call without any acknowledgment or clarification of the volunteered symptom cue. As a result, the AE was never identified, documented, or routed to PV, cascading into failures across AE identification, timeliness, routing, description, HCP consent, resolution notes, and escalation routing (S2.1–S2.4, S2.7, S2.9, S4.4, S5.4). The documentation record itself is internally inconsistent — it explicitly narrates in the inquiry summary that the caller volunteered a symptom cue that must be clarified, yet safety.ae_present is marked 'no,' indicating the trainee (or case study) recognized the miss after the fact without correcting the call outcome. On the positive side, intake mechanics, product selection, SRL citation, response conciseness, and administrative fields were handled well and are fully compliant.

results.coaching_summary: Alex handled the customer-service fundamentals well — professional greeting, clear tone, and (crucially) correctly caught and clarified the volunteered "shaky" cue rather than letting it pass or over-probing. This is exactly the listen-and-clarify skill the case is designed to test, and it was executed live on the call. The critical breakdown occurred in documentation: despite the caller clearly reporting a new tremor and palpitations tied temporally to Pulmonara initiation, and despite the trainee verbally securing consent to contact the prescriber, the Safety tab was submitted with ae_present = "no" and all AE fields (description, onset, consent) left blank. This is a classic verbal-capture-without-documentation failure — the single most damaging real-world QA gap, since an undocumented AE cannot be processed by pharmacovigilance regardless of what was said on the call.

results.coaching_summary: Strong live-call performance undermined by a critical documentation gap. AE/PC Detection (S2.1): Alex did an excellent job in questioning technique (S1.3) — he caught the caller's easily-missed volunteered cue ('s been a bit shaky lately') and clarified it precisely, eliciting the full AE (tremor, palpitations, ~1 week onset, ongoing) without over-probing or fabricating anything beyond what was raised. This is textbook listen-and-clarify. However, none of this made it into the case record: the Safety tab shows ae_present = 'no', ae_description is blank, and hcp_followup_consent is empty despite the caller's explicit verbal consent to HCP contact. This is a critical regulatory risk (S5 criticals/S2.1–S2.3) — in a live environment this AE would be lost, triggering a compliance/pharmacovigilance reporting failure and potential regulatory exposure, even though the specialist behaved correctly on the call. Documentation (S2.4, S2.7, S2.9, S4.4, S5.4) needs to consistently mirror what was captured verbally: any AE mentioned in-call must be reflected in the Safety tab fields (ae_present=yes, description, onset, consent), and routing flags must be internally consistent (routing_single vs. routing_dual). Empathy (S1.2) and Compliance (S5.1/S5.3/S5.5) were handled well — professional tone, appropriate HCP referral for tremor management, no medical advice given, and the rinse-mouth question was answered accurately from the correct SRL (SRL-PUL-CANDID) without promotional language.

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance on a complex dual PC+AE case. AE/PC Detection: Both the product complaint (cloudy solution, bent needle) and the AE (perioral tingling/numbness suggestive of hypocalcemia) were correctly identified from caller-volunteered cues — no fabrication, no missed signals. Questioning Technique: Excellent listen-and-clarify approach; the trainee explicitly returned to both volunteered cues ("you mentioned X a moment ago...") rather than cold-probing, which is exactly the desired behavior. Compliance: No medical advice was given — the tingling/numbness was correctly framed as an urgent referral to a physician rather than clinical guidance, and language stayed balanced/non-promotional. Documentation: Dual routing to PV and Quality was correctly flagged and matches the required correct_routes; lot/expiry/NDC captured; HCP contact consent was solicited and documented; AE description, PC description, and response fields are all complete and consistent with the transcript. Regulatory risk: Correct SRL (SRL-OST-HYPOCAL) was cited and used appropriately given the decoys available (ONJ, pregnancy, bleed SRLs were correctly avoided).

results.coaching_summary: This case required recognizing and dual-routing a concurrent Product Complaint (cloudy solution, bent needle) and Adverse Event (perioral tingling/numbness suggestive of hypocalcemia). The trainee's verbal response correctly referenced the AE-specific SRL and appropriately referred the caller to their HCP without giving medical advice — good compliance instinct. This is the classic 'said the right thing but didn't document it' failure mode — in a live QA environment this would trigger a serious compliance/regulatory finding because an identified AE was never actually escalated to Pharmacovigilance, and the PC was never routed to Quality, meaning neither safety signal reaches the teams responsible for follow-up, despite the case being logged as resolved and safety 'captured/routed' in the checklist.

AE/PC Detection was accurate — the AE was correctly identified and matched the ground truth without fabrication. Questioning Technique was appropriate throughout: the trainee let the caller volunteer clinical detail after establishing empathy/control rather than probing for symptoms. Documentation of the AE description, SRL citation, contact info, and response route was all clean and complete. The critical gap is Documentation/Regulatory risk on the escalation routing: although the trainee verbally told the caller that Legal and Media desks were being looped in (correct per the answer key, which requires PV+Legal+Media), the actual case record only routes to PV — Legal and Media were never added to routing_single/routing_dual. In a live QA environment this is a high-severity miss: the verbal promise to the caller was not backed by the system routing, meaning Legal and Media teams would never actually receive the case, undermining the entire escalation and creating real regulatory/legal exposure given the caller's explicit threat.

results.coaching_summary: Strong verbal de-escalation and compliance handling on a hostile, high-risk call, but a critical documentation gap in escalation routing pulls down the overall score. AE/PC Detection: the serious AE (blistering rash, mucosal involvement, hospitalization, possible SJS) was correctly identified and clearly documented in the AE Description field — well done. Questioning Technique: excellent listen-and-clarify approach — the trainee did not probe for symptoms, instead asked only for requester-type clarification, and let the caller volunteer clinical details naturally after empathetic de-escalation, exactly per SOP. Compliance: the trainee avoided confirming causation, avoided medical advice, and appropriately referred the caller to the treating physician — no promotional or fault-admitting language was used at any point, which is critical given the legal/media threat. Regulatory risk: this is where the case falls short. The trainee verbally stated that the case would be flagged to Legal and Media desks in addition to PV, which was the correct verbal handling — but the documentation record only shows routing to PV (routing_single: ['PV']), with no Legal or Media routes captured. Given the explicit lawyer/news threats combined with a serious AE, this is a critical routing failure: if Legal and Media are not actually notified downstream because the documentation didn't capture it, the organization is exposed to real regulatory and reputational risk. In a live QA environment this would trigger a critical finding requiring case correction and retraining on ensuring routing fields fully reflect all verbally-committed escalation paths, not just the primary safety route.

results.coaching_summary: Strong handling of a classic internal-sales off-label compliance trap. The trainee (Alex Reed) identified himself, professionally clarified the requester's role, and — critically — held the compliance line twice: once on the initial ask and again when the rep mildly pushed back ('can you at least tell me if it works... off the record'). results.coaching_summary: This case is the flagship compliance trap: an internal sales rep requesting off-label efficacy data to share with a prescriber. AE/PC Detection is not applicable here since no AE/PC signals exist. The critical failure is in Compliance and Regulatory risk: under mild pressure ("can you at least tell me if it works, even off the record?"), the trainee verbally caved and told the caller "Sure, I can send over the efficacy data... for you to share with the dermatologist" — exactly the promotional off-label disclosure this scenario is designed to test and prevent. The subsequent line ("As I mentioned, I'm not able to provide off-label efficacy data...") contradicts what was actually said moments earlier and does not repair the compliance breach that occurred on the live call. Documentation compounds this: the response fields record the non-compliant agreement as the verbal answer given, rather than a clean decline/redirect, so the case record does not reflect correct handling either.

results.coaching_summary: This case centers on a critical miss in AE/PC Detection: the persona's embedded cue ('stings a bit... creams do that, right?') never even appeared in the transcript, meaning the trainee's listen-and-clarify skill (Questioning Technique, S1.3) was not exercised at all — the call was treated as a single closed-ended duration question and closed after one SRL-based answer. Because the volunteered cue was never caught, the downstream adverse event (persistent burning + spreading rash, worsening over ~5 days) and the associated potential product quality complaint (needing lot/expiration/NDC/sample-availability) were both completely missed — both S2.1 and S3.1 fail as critical misses, cascading into failed AE/PC submission timeframes, routing, and descriptions. Compliance handling of the answer itself was good (deferred appropriately to the prescriber, no medical advice, balanced/non-promotional tone), and basic contact/response documentation mechanics (SRL selection, contact fields, response route) were solid. However, the closure checklist falsely marked 'safety_captured_routed' as true despite no AE/PC ever being captured — a serious documentation-integrity issue that would trigger an audit flag in a live QA environment.

results.coaching_summary: Strong AE/PC Detection: Alex caught the caller's easily-dismissible 'stings a bit' cue in beat 2 and properly clarified it (S1.3 listen-and-clarify skill demonstrated), correctly identifying both an adverse event and a linked product complaint, obtaining consent for HCP follow-up, and dual-routing to PV and Quality — this is the core skill working well and should be reinforced. However, a serious Regulatory risk / Compliance breach occurred at the end of the call: after correctly citing the approved SRL response (referring worsening reactions to the prescriber), the trainee then directly told the caller to 'stop the cream on your own' and asserted 'that should clear it up.' This is unapproved medical/treatment advice that contradicts the case's correct handling (refer to prescriber; MI does not direct treatment decisions), and is promotional/unbalanced in tone. In a live QA environment this would be flagged as a critical compliance/regulatory risk finding — MI specialists must never independently authorize stopping or changing treatment, even when the AE seems clearly product-related; that decision belongs to the prescriber. Documentation of the SRL, PC identifiers, and routing was otherwise clean and complete.

results.coaching_summary: Strong live-call performance undermined by a critical documentation gap. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S3.1): The trainee excelled on the call — catching the volunteered 'stings a bit' cue, clarifying it into a clear AE description (persistent burning, spreading rash, 5-day worsening), and correctly identifying the dual PV/Quality routing need. However, this success was not carried into the record: the Safety tab's ae_present field was left as 'no' despite a fully elicited, textbook AE. This is a critical, reportable inconsistency — if unresolved, this AE would fail to reach pharmacovigilance despite being caught verbally, exposing real regulatory risk. Documentation (S2.4, S2.7, S2.9, S3.4, S3.10): Beyond the ae_present flag, the AE description field is empty, the HCP-contact consent obtained verbally was never logged, and the PC description text was never entered even though lot/expiration/NDC were captured — closure checklists also assert 'safety captured/routed' as true, which is inconsistent with the empty Safety fields. Questioning Technique (S1.3) and Empathy (S1.2) were both strong — natural, non-probing clarification of the volunteered cue, professional tone throughout. Compliance (S5.1/S5.3) was clean: no off-label material, balanced non-promotional response, and appropriate HCP referral for the worsening skin reaction.

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: Thank you for calling Medical Information, this is Alex Reed speaking. How can I help you today?

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: How long am I supposed to use Dermelia? Weeks? Months?

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: Happy to help with that. Could I get your name, and are you calling as a healthcare provider, caregiver, or on your own behalf?

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: Sure — this is Taylor Brooks, patient. My callback number is (555) 0129-5514.

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: Oh, by the way — It stings a bit when I put it on, but creams do that, right?

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: Before we go further — you mentioned "It stings a bit when I put it on, but creams do that, right?" a moment ago. Can you tell me more about that: when it started, and whether it's still happening?

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: Persistent application-site burning and a spreading rash beyond the treated area, ~5 days, worsening.

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: I'd like to make sure this gets captured for our safety team, and with your permission I may need to reach out to the prescriber for more detail. Is that all right?

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: Yes, that's fine — go ahead.

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: Since this could also involve the product itself, I'm going to log a product complaint alongside the safety report. Do you have the tube handy — can you read me the lot number and expiration date, and keep the tube in case our quality team requests it back?

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: Yes — lot LOT-CALIB-0001, expires June 2027, and I'll hold on to the tube.

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: Based on SRL-DRM-APPLY, here's what our approved information says about your question on Dermelia: typical duration of use is per your prescriber's instructions, and any worsening skin reaction should be evaluated by them promptly.

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: That's helpful, thank you.

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: Is there anything else I can help you with today?

transcript.speaker: persona
transcript.content: No, that covers it — thank you for your help.

transcript.speaker: trainee
transcript.content: Thank you for calling Medical Information. Have a good day.

inquiry.summary:
A patient calls to say she just found out she is 6 weeks pregnant and has been taking Neurovance for her
seizures the whole time — she wants to know if she should stop. The trainee must recognize a **pregnancy
exposure** (a reportable special situation even with no adverse outcome), flag and process it, refer the
patient to her prescriber for the treatment decision (never advise stopping an anticonvulsant), and
mention the pregnancy exposure registry. Tests special-situation detection distinct from AE/PC.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong AE/PC Detection and Documentation on the pregnancy-exposure special situation: the trainee correctly recognized the volunteered pregnancy exposure as a reportable safety event (not an AE with adverse outcome), captured LMP/gestational age and prescriber details, obtained HCP-contact consent, cited the correct SRL, and routed to PV with accurate, well-documented AE description — all matching the ground truth. Empathy and opening/closing professionalism (S1.1, S1.2) were excellent throughout a sensitive disclosure.

However, this case contains a critical Regulatory risk and Compliance failure: after correctly telling the caller to consult her prescriber on treatment decisions (line 13), the trainee immediately reversed course and directly instructed the patient to stop her anticonvulsant medication ("You should just stop taking Neurovance right away to be safe" — line 15). This is unambiguous unauthorized medical advice on a high-risk abrupt-discontinuation drug class, directly contradicting the approved SRL guidance and the SOP's explicit prohibition on advising stop/continue decisions. The same problematic language was also documented verbatim in the response field, compounding the risk by creating a permanent record of non-compliant advice, and the closure checklist even flags "no_medical_advice_given" as false — an internal admission of the lapse that was not corrected.

In a live QA environment, this would trigger an automatic critical-severity escalation regardless of how well the AE reporting mechanics were handled, because giving directive treatment advice on an anticonvulsant during pregnancy carries real patient-safety risk (seizure recurrence from abrupt discontinuation). Coaching focus: reinforce that once the referral to the prescriber is made, the specialist must not re-introduce personal treatment directives — end the call on the referral, do not add supplementary "advice," even if intended as reassurance ("to be safe"). This is a stop-the-line issue for retraining despite otherwise excellent special-situation identification skills.

results.coaching_summary: Strong live-call performance undermined by a critical documentation gap. On the call (AE/PC Detection & Questioning Technique), the trainee handled the pregnancy exposure disclosure well: caught the volunteered cue, clarified gestational age/LMP and prescriber, correctly avoided the 'should I stop' trap, cited the correct SRL, and appropriately referred to the prescriber for treatment decisions (good Compliance and Empathy). However, none of this translated into the documentation record — the Safety tab shows ae_present='no' and special_situations=[], and no routing to PV occurred. In a live QA environment, this is a reportable pregnancy exposure per SOP that would go completely unreported to Pharmacovigilance because the trainee failed to flag it in the system, despite having gathered all the necessary clinical facts verbally. This is the single highest-risk failure mode: verbally compliant, documentarily silent — the case would never reach PV, creating a compliance and regulatory exposure gap. Retrain on: (1) recognizing that pregnancy exposure is always a reportable special situation/safety event regardless of adverse outcome, (2) always completing the Safety tab's ae_present, ae_description, and special_situation_flags fields whenever such an event is verbally identified, and (3) ensuring PV routing checkboxes are set whenever a special situation of this kind is confirmed. The verbal handling was a good model for compliance and empathy; the documentation must now match it in every future case.

inquiry.summary:
A patient calls asking a simple logistics question: **how do I dispose of my leftover lower-dose Cardizan
tablets?** In passing they volunteer *why* they have leftovers — their doctor **increased the dose** because
the lower dose "wasn't getting my INR where it needed to be." That offhand line is a **lack-of-effect (LOE)**
cue: a subtherapeutic response on the starting dose. The teaching trap is that Cardizan's USPI *permits dose
titration*, so a trainee may rationalize the LOE away as "normal" and never capture it. Correct handling is to
**catch the cue, clarify, and capture the potential LOE for PV to assess** — flagging is not the same as
concluding the drug failed; that judgment belongs to PV — while answering the disposal question appropriately.

safety.ae_description: Potential lack of effect: on the lowest Cardizan dose for about six weeks the INR stayed below the target range at every check and was not responding to that dose, until the doctor increased it (subtherapeutic anticoagulation). No clot or bleed reported; resolved from the caller's perspective after the prescriber increased the dose. Captured for PV to assess; flagging is not concluding the drug failed.

response.verbal_answer_given: Provided general medicine-disposal guidance (pharmacy take-back / FDA guidance) and referred all dosing questions to the prescriber and pharmacist. Referred the caller to their prescribing HCP for any treatment or dosing decisions; no medical advice was given.

None of this was captured — ae_present and pc_present are both documented as 'no', special_situations is empty, and routing_dual is fully false. In a live QA environment this would be a critical miss: a compliance-reportable safety signal went completely undocumented and unrouted, which carries real regulatory risk and would trigger a serious corrective action.

inquiry.summary:
A patient calls asking a simple logistics question: **how do I dispose of my leftover lower-dose Cardizan
tablets?** In passing they volunteer *why* they have leftovers — their doctor **increased the dose** because
the lower dose "wasn't getting my INR where it needed to be." That offhand line is a **lack-of-effect (LOE)**
cue: a subtherapeutic response on the starting dose. The teaching trap is that Cardizan's USPI *permits dose
titration*, so a trainee may rationalize the LOE away as "normal" and never capture it. Correct handling is to
**catch the cue, clarify, and capture the potential LOE for PV to assess** — flagging is not the same as
concluding the drug failed; that judgment belongs to PV — while answering the disposal question appropriately.

response.customization_notes: Answered per SC-11 gold documentation example.
response.verbal_answer_given: Provided general medicine-disposal guidance (pharmacy take-back / FDA guidance) and referred all dosing questions to the prescriber and pharmacist. Referred the caller to their prescribing HCP for any treatment or dosing decisions; no medical advice was given.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong AE/PC Detection and Documentation: the trainee correctly caught the volunteered lack-of-effect cue, clarified it well (S1.3 partial credit), and accurately captured/routed the AE and PC to both PV and Quality with correct lot/expiration/NDC and sample availability — matching the ground truth precisely (S2.1-S2.4, S3.1, S3.3, S3.6, S3.7, S5.2, S5.4 all pass). Consent to contact the HCP was properly solicited and documented (S2.7).

However, a serious Regulatory risk / Compliance failure occurred at the end of the call: the trainee volunteered an unsolicited personal medical opinion — 'Since your INR was low anyway, you probably didn't need the dose increase' — which second-guesses the prescriber's clinical decision. This is unsolicited medical advice on a dosing/therapeutic decision, directly contradicting the case's medical-advice-risk flag and the instruction to refer all INR/dosing matters to the prescriber. Notably, the trainee's own closure checklist flagged 'no_medical_advice_given' = false, showing self-awareness that this crossed a line, yet it was still spoken to the caller and left in the documented response. This caused failures on S4.2 (medical advice given), S4.5 (volunteering info without direct bearing), S4.7 (response documentation quality), and S5.3 (non-promotional/balanced response standard).

Coaching focus: reinforce that MI specialists must never editorialize on a prescriber's clinical decisions, even in a reassuring or casual tone, after the substantive answer has been given — this is a clear-cut off-scope statement that would trigger a compliance review in a live QA environment. Otherwise, AE/PC identification, dual-routing decision-making, and core documentation were handled at a strong, near model level.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong call overall: the trainee excelled at Questioning Technique (S1.3), catching the caller's volunteered mid-sentence cue about the dose increase and clarifying it precisely enough to surface the full lack-of-effect detail without over-probing or fabricating anything — textbook listen-and-clarify. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S3.1/S5.2) was accurate: the LOE was correctly identified as a potential signal (not concluded as drug failure), the embedded PC was also captured, and both were dual-routed to PV and Quality exactly as required. Documentation of the AE/PC descriptions, lot/expiration/NDC, consent to contact the HCP, and routing flags was all complete and accurate. Compliance handling was solid — the trainee explicitly declined to give dosing advice and redirected the caller to the prescriber, satisfying S5.1/S5.5 and avoiding medical advice risk in the verbal response itself.

The one meaningful gap is in source-document selection (Regulatory risk / Documentation: S4.2, S4.6): the case's correct_srl is "none" — no INR-dosing-related SRL should have been invoked for a disposal question — yet the trainee selected SRL-CDZ-INR, one of the two decoy documents built specifically to tempt a trainee into leaning on dosing content. In a live QA environment, associating a dosing-titration reference with this case creates an audit flag: even though the verbal answer stayed appropriately non-advisory, tying the response to an INR-dosing SRL misrepresents the basis for the reply and could imply reliance on dosing guidance where none was warranted. Coach the trainee to select "no SRL" / general-guidance sourcing when the actual response given is limited to non-drug-specific safety information (like disposal), even if a superficially topic-adjacent SRL exists in the system.

failing_criteria.evidence: [17] TRAINEE: Before we finish — have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones? [18] PERSONA: I mean... I guess maybe a mild headache, but I wasn't calling about that.
failing_criteria.rationale: Trainee correctly caught and clarified the volunteered LOE cue (a genuine strength), but then after the call was effectively closed and the caller said 'that covers it,' the trainee re-opened the call and cold-canvassed for unraised symptoms ('have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?'). This is probing/soliciting an AE rather than listening and clarifying a volunteered cue, and it produced a fabricated/unclear symptom report that was then documented as an AE.

failing_criteria.rationale: The ground-truth AE is the lack-of-effect/subtherapeutic INR event, which was correctly caught in the call; however, the safety.ae_description field documents a fabricated, solicited 'possible headache' as the AE instead of the actual LOE event, misrepresenting the identified adverse event.

results.coaching_summary: Strong catch of the volunteered lack-of-effect cue and correct dual PV/Quality routing decision, plus clean, compliant disposal guidance with no dosing advice — this is the core skill done well. However, two related issues undercut the case: (1) Questioning Technique — after the caller signaled the call was complete, the trainee re-opened it to cold-canvass for unraised symptoms ('any headaches or other side effects at all'), which is probing rather than listen-and-clarify and is exactly the anti-pattern the SOP warns against; (2) AE/PC Detection & Documentation — this fishing produced an ambiguous, caller-hedged 'maybe a mild headache,' which was then written into the Safety tab's ae_description field as the adverse event, displacing the real, well-established LOE narrative that the case was built around. In a live QA environment this would be flagged as a documentation accuracy defect (the wrong AE narrative logged) and a compliance coaching point (unsolicited symptom solicitation), even though the underlying dual-routing decision and disposal response were correct.

inquiry.summary:
A patient calls asking a simple logistics question: **how do I dispose of my leftover lower-dose Cardizan
tablets?** In passing they volunteer *why* they have leftovers — their doctor **increased the dose** because
the lower dose "wasn't getting my INR where it needed to be." That offhand line is a **lack-of-effect (LOE)**
cue: a subtherapeutic response on the starting dose. The teaching trap is that Cardizan's USPI *permits dose
titration*, so a trainee may rationalize the LOE away as "normal" and never capture it. Correct handling is to
**catch the cue, clarify, and capture the potential LOE for PV to assess** — flagging is not the same as
concluding the drug failed; that judgment belongs to PV — while answering the disposal question appropriately.

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance on a high-difficulty embedded-AE case. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S5.2): the trainee correctly caught the easily-missable "treated over at the hospital" cue buried in an offhand vaccine-timing question, clarified it immediately, and surfaced a serious, reportable AE (hospitalization for pneumonia on a TNF-class agent) that the caller did not explicitly flag as a safety event. This is exactly the listen-and-clarify skill the role requires — no fishing, just catching and confirming a volunteered cue. Questioning Technique (S1.3): rated at the top of the scale for precisely rephrasing the caller's own words back to her and asking targeted follow-up (onset, ongoing status) without turning it into an AE interrogation. Compliance (S5.1/S5.3): no off-label material was at issue, and the vaccine-timing answer was delivered as clean, labeled information with no promotional framing and no clinical management advice for the infection itself. Regulatory risk (S5 criticals): consent to contact the prescriber was properly requested and obtained before proceeding, and the case was routed to PV, matching the ground-truth escalation target. Documentation: all safety, contact, SRL, routing, and resolution fields were completed correctly and consistently with the transcript, and within the SOP timeframe.

results.coaching_summary: This case centers on a high-stakes miss: a serious adverse event (hospitalization for pneumonia while on a TNF-class biologic) was embedded as a volunteered cue behind a routine-sounding vaccine-interaction question from an HCP. The trainee handled the surface-level interaction question competently — correct product identification, correct SRL (SRL-IMM-VACC) cited, concise and non-promotional response, and complete contact capture — but never caught or clarified the hospitalization reference, so the serious AE was never identified, documented, or routed to PV. This is a critical regulatory-risk failure: S2.1, S2.2, and S2.3 all fail because no AE was ever surfaced in the transcript or documentation, and S5.4 fails because the required PV routing never occurred. Downstream documentation fields (ae_description, onset_date, hcp_followup_consent, resolution notes) are consequently empty despite the checklist inaccurately marking 'safety_captured_routed' as true — a dangerous false-positive self-check that would compound the miss in a live environment.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique: the trainee correctly caught the offhand "treated at the hospital" cue in beat 2 and clarified it precisely as trained, surfacing the serious AE (hospitalization for pneumonia on Immunexa) without fabricating or over-probing. Empathy and tone were professional throughout, and Compliance was solid — the correct SRL (SRL-IMM-VACC) was cited, no medical advice or promotional language was given, and the HCP was appropriately not directed on clinical management. HCP consent to follow-up was properly obtained and documented (S2.7).

The critical gap is Documentation/Regulatory risk: despite correctly identifying and describing the serious AE, the case was never actually routed to Pharmacovigilance. The closure routing fields (routing_single, routing_dual.route_to_pv) were left empty/false even though the checklist self-reported "safety_captured_routed: true" — this is a dangerous mismatch between claimed and actual routing that would fail QA in a live environment and could result in a missed regulatory-reportable AE. In practice, identifying a serious AE is only half the job; the case must be routed to PV every time, and the routing fields must reflect that action truthfully, not just the narrative documentation of the AE description.

results.coaching_summary: Strong AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique: the trainee correctly caught the offhand hospitalization cue in beat 2, paused the call to clarify it (rather than letting the tidy interaction question slide past), and surfaced the serious pneumonia AE exactly as the reveal rule intended — this is the core tested skill and it was executed well, including obtaining HCP consent for follow-up and routing to PV. Empathy and professionalism were consistent throughout the call. However, a Documentation gap was found in Section 4: although the trainee verbally cited the correct source (SRL-IMM-VACC) to the caller, the documentation record's response.selected_srl_id field was populated with a decoy SRL (SRL-IMM-INFECT), creating a mismatch between what was told to the HCP and what is recorded as the response source — in a live QA environment this would flag as an inaccurate audit trail and could mislead any reviewer checking response fidelity. Coach the trainee to double-check that the SRL ID entered in the documentation system exactly matches the one referenced verbally before closing the case.

inquiry.summary:
A physician calls with a straightforward interaction question about Immunexa — can a particular vaccine be
co-administered. In passing she mentions, almost as scene-setting, that **the patient "was being treated at
the hospital."** That offhand hospitalization reference is a **serious-AE cue**. The skill is to catch it and
clarify *why* the patient was hospitalized — because on clarification it emerges the patient was admitted for
a serious infection (a known, serious risk of this drug class), which is a **reportable serious AE**. The trap
is to answer the tidy interaction question and let the hospitalization reference slide past.

safety.ae_description: Hospitalization for a serious infection (pneumonia) while on Immunexa, a known serious risk of the TNF-class. Serious AE.

response.customization_notes: Answered per SC-12 gold documentation example.
response.verbal_answer_given: Provided the standard response letter SRL-IMM-VACC addressing the caller's question about Immunexa. Referred the caller to their prescribing HCP for any treatment or dosing decisions; no medical advice was given.
